data.title: GXO Logistics: A Buy Call With Rising Macro Risks
data.description:
In October 2025, I upgraded GXO Logistics (GXO) from hold to buy. Since then, the stock has lost 4.8% of its value against the S&P 500 losing 5.6% of its value. That share price decline is driven by the situation in the Middle East, which provides risk for companies in the freight and logistics industry. While the performance is not in line with my expectations for a buy rated name, I do believe the stock price decline has been relatively modest. In this report, I discuss the pressures that GXO Logistics may face, the earnings for 2025 and outlook for 2026 along with a price target update as I maintain my buy rating.

Situation In The Middle East May Affect GXO Logistics

The escalation in the Middle East has sent energy prices up and that is the nightmare for many freight and logistics companies. For GXO Logistics, the impact of higher energy prices is somewhat limited. GXO is a contract logistics provider. So, any higher transportation it may face for inbound and outbound warehouse traffic flows can be passed through with a small mark up. At the same time, higher energy prices may result in higher energy costs in the warehouses against which I believe GXO Logistics is less shielded. Quite often the higher energy costs can be passed through, meaning that the risk of higher energy prices is somewhat limited to margin friction caused by pass-throughs not being instant.

The bigger problem for GXO Logistics is on the demand side. Higher energy prices may result in inflation, increased uncertainty and weaker consumer demand meaning that demand for freight and logistics services is pressured. That is actually the main risk for GXO Logistics at this point.

The company is partially insulated against increasing energy prices and inflation, but not against erosion of demand driven by weaker consumer demand.

Margin Decline For GXO In 2025

In 2025, GXO posted sales of $13.2 billion with 3.9% organic growth and 12.5% total growth. Adjusted EBITDA rose 8% to $881 million with adjusted EBITDA margins dropping 30 basis points to 6.7%. These are not awful margins for a contracts logistics provider, but they are not huge either. Operating margins remained at 1.9% and that is definitely too low. Those margins should be around 3%-5%. The company is adding new business and while that is a good thing, new business often comes with thing margins initially with GXO having to work to improve their efficiency.

GXO Expected Margin Uplift Could Be Eliminated

For 2026, the company guided for 4%-5% organic growth with adjusted EBITDA at $930-$970 million marking 7.8% growth. Overall, margins should expand around 20 basis points. That is not a huge increase and can easily be lost in the current turmoil. We also note that the guidance was issued on February 10th along with the earnings release, which means that the guidance does not incorporate any impact from the war. So, I do believe that overall GXO Logistics could be facing some margin challenges. At the same time, when the macro backdrop is weak it does provide opportunities to acquire companies providing opportunities for improved operating leverage and grow as a company to give GXO Logistics more pricing power in a highly fragmented market.

GXO Logistics Stock Still A Buy Despite Risks

To determine multi-year price targets The Aerospace Forum has developed a stock screener that uses a combination of analyst consensus on EBITDA, cash flows and the most recent balance sheet data. Each quarter, we revisit those assumptions and the stock price targets accordingly. In a separate blog, I have detailed our analysis methodology.

I believe that despite the macro risks, GXO Logistics is still a buy for the simple reason that the company traded at an EV/EBITDA median of 13.6x over the past years which is below its longer-term EV/EBITDA and that still provides 15% upside against the already realized earnings for 2025. The company is also working on adding new customers in higher value sectors which should help the company to gain better business. Against 2026 expected earnings which do carry some uncertainty, there is 38% upside.

Sales growth at 5.6% annually through 2028 is definitely not spectacular but could be supplemented by acquisitions. EBITDA growth is looking a whole lot better at 9.6% annually with free cash flow growth of 23.4%. GXO Logistics cannot control market demand, but the company has a nice growth set up if it can meet sales estimates.

Margins are expected to improve substantially as the company gains scale and new business. Capital expenditures are expected to remain manageable with positive free cash flow that should help the company reduce its net debt leverage. GXO has a $500 million share repurchase program of which $300 million still remains available. I have currently modelled that into the share repurchases for 2026 and 2027. However, I do believe that the company would be better served by bringing its net debt leverage down or consider additional M&A opportunities to drive operating leverage or accelerate automation in its warehouses.

Conclusion: GXO Logistics Has Appeal Even With A Challenging Backdrop

GXO Logistics does not have margins that look impressive compared to peers and the macro risks are currently increasing. However, we note that the company is adding higher value customers through customer additions in aerospace and defense, life sciences and datacenters with AI and automation providing a partial decoupling of labor from volumes which also allows the company to execute existing contracts at higher margins which could ultimately lead to the company having more funds available to acquire businesses that it can integrate to create a more dominant market position. So, while there are risks I am maintaining my buy rating for the time being.

data.title: Crude Oil Keeps Playing Tricks On Markets 32 Days Into The Iran War
data.description:
By Elior Manier

President Trump has just finished speaking in yet another public address, boasting that the ongoing conflict is so efficient and revolutionary, and comparing the long-lasting historical conflicts in the United States with the current advancement in objectives.

But markets don't care anymore. What they want to see is a proper solution for the Strait of Hormuz.

Despite strong reactions to his speeches during the first days of the war, traders and algorithms have progressively stopped reacting to any overly optimistic announcement from the Administration. As a matter of fact, reactions to them are now doing the exact opposite.

After his speech at the White House yesterday, global assets began to tumble from a sweep higher in energy commodities. Crude prices rose in a flash from $100 to $114 (WTI). While participants were becoming more hopeful that the conflict would end within the early-announced deadlines (5 weeks, then April 6), a more aggressive tone led to a market-shaking explosion, as the party quickly finished and left a general hangover.

Combine the worsening tone with high-tier catalysts ahead, like tomorrow's Non-Farm Payrolls, closed stock markets (only futures will be open until 1:30 PM ET), and the potential for an escalation, including a ground invasion over the long weekend, and that was enough of a hit to blow up the tires from the bull train.

Talk won't be enough to soothe markets in the long term. Oil is what smart money is looking to move their chess pieces in this gigantic geopolitical puzzle.

Hence, let's dive right into an intraday outlook for both WTI and Brent oil, highlighting their technical levels and outlining scenarios for their breakouts or breakdowns.

Crude Oil Market Check and Technical Levels ahead of the Long Weekend

WTI 4H Chart

Evolving in two different bull channels, the larger one is less reactive but more concerning, pointing to the potential of another top at $120 if bears fail to correct prices.

The second bull channel, of smaller scale, would see a potential top having already been formed, and would see its bottom at $100. RSI is forming a bearish divergence, which could prompt this smaller channel to hold.

As long as WTI remains above $100, investors won't be able to generate much progress in sentiment. Tomorrow's Non-Farm Payrolls shouldn't have much effect on oil, but the general weekend risk will. Hence, traders will be listening closely to the advancement of the war.

WTI Technical Levels:

Resistance Levels

  • Daily highs $113.50-114.50 (small channel top)
  • 2022 and Monday highs $117-120 (larger channel top)
  • Ukraine war spike $120-124

Support Levels

  • $106-108 June 2022 pivot
  • $98-100 momentum support and 4H 50-period MA (bearish below)
  • Pivotal support $93.00-95
  • $82.80-84 key support
  • War flows pivot $65.00-66.00

Brent 4H Chart

The range is now consolidating in a tighter trading between $102 and $114, which brings more definite breakout levels.

  • Above the mini-range resistance ($111-114), expect further market stress.
  • Below $100-102, however, expect sentiment to rebound swiftly.

The worst-case scenario is avoided in markets as long as Brent does not break the war spike at $120. After this, expect a catastrophic price action and rate hikes pricing to continue.

Brent Technical Levels:

Resistance Levels

  • Range resistance $111-114
  • War highs $117-120
  • Ukraine war spike $130-135

Support Levels

  • $100-102 end-March and range support
  • End-March minor support $95-97
  • $88-92 March 10 bounce and 200-MA
  • $80-82 key war support
  • Pre-war gap $75

Keep track of the headlines and watch out for large gaps and sweeps in coming periods (with many players absent for the Easter long weekend).

Safe trades!

Original Post

data.title: Why I Am Rating SanDisk A Strong Buy
data.description:
Investment Thesis

I believe SanDisk Corporation (SNDK) is uniquely positioned to benefit from the exploding demand of flash storage. The AI boom and Cloud buildout have turned NAND chips into a hot commodity I would say. Supply is tight while hyperscalers keep raising budgets. SNDK's CEO notes that data center demand is rising faster than supply. And this pushes the data center segment to become the largest NAND market in 2026. The company is locking in multi year contracts with hyperscalers. And this is in order to capture this increasing surge. At the same time Sandisk keeps R&D focused on AI. And this is because it is developing a 128TB data center and a novel high bandwidth with SK Hynix (HXSC.F) as noted by the CEO:

...And then we haven't launched our Stargate product yet for the storage-based QLC, it's in qualification. We'll start shipping that for revenue in the next couple of quarters, which we're excited about, providing another tailwind to growth to our data center portfolio. And that will up the mix of QLC. But at this point, I think the overall market in our portfolio is -- it's tilted towards TLC...

In simple terms, Sandisk has a superior product offering, low costs and pricing power in a tight market.

I am rating Sandisk Corporation a Strong Buy because of a couple of reasons. First among the reasons behind my thesis is the 2Q 2026 earnings results. The company handily beat guidance. Because revenue was reported at $3.03Bn while gross margin increased from 30% to 50% as favorable mix and pricing kicked in. Also, data center revenue in the quarter was $440Mn. I also note that the company is now forecasting between $4.4Bn and $4.8Bn for 3Q 2026. This implies that there is continued robust growth. I also note that SNDK reaffirmed a mid to high teens annual growth.

Business Overview

The semiconductor industry is quite cyclical but right now fundamentals are distorted by AI. Traditionally, consumer devices drove NAND demand. But according to SNDK data center customers became the largest customers of NAND. This means that every chip maker is scrambling to supply AI servers. And this means that pricing has rebounded. In fact, according to SNDK's CFO, the NAND market is undergoing a structural evolution that is catalyzed by AI. With accelerating storage content per deployment.

When I look at Sandisk's large scale focus on high end SSDs, then I note that this gives it a cost/quality edge over peers. According to an article done on SNDK, I note that the author thinks that Sandisk can now behave like an infrastructure player with fatter margins and not the usual cyclical memory OEM. And this is what I would call the edge in computing. And I say this in refence to SNDK's Growth. I mean the company has a revenue growth of 23.60% y/y while the sector median stands at 9.92%.

Growth Drivers

First and foremost, one of the key growth drivers for SNDK is the AI data center buildout and supply strategy. The company has secured long term contracts with cloud giants to balance predictable demand with attractive economics. These deals are meant to ensure that SNDK is not chasing short term price swings. I also believe that it gives Sandisk priority access in a tight market. The company highlights that "...customers willing to make longer term commitments will receive priority allocation..." And as a result of this, there are some customers that are planning to 2028 and even negotiating 1 to 5 years supplies.

On the product side, Sandisk is already shipping new high end SSDs and preparting next gen chips. For instance, 128TB stargate QLC SSD hyperscalers has already completed qualification process. Yet the company is prototyping a high bandwidth flash cell [in collaboration with SK Hynix] that is tailored to AI inference. And this aims for 10x density gains. I also note that SNDK's existing business is also scaling. And I say this because 2Q data revenue increased by 76% and edge revenue increased 63%. On the other side, consumers such as phones and cameras grew by 52%. This of course shows broad strength. And it means that all segments enjoy enjoying supply shortages mean favorable pricing.

Valuation

Earlier I mentioned that SNDK has its revenue growth rate higher than the sector median. Well, when I look at the Valuation multiples, a couple of things are quite clear. First, the company has its FWD non-GAAP P/E at 15.78x while the sector median 21.28x. This shows that there is a 25.86% discount relative to the sector median. Also, the FWD EV/EBITDA stands at 11.92x while the sector median trades at 12.71x. This shows a 6.2% discount relative to the sector median. What catches my attention is the FWD non-GAAP PEG. And this is important to me because it adjusts for growth. SNDK has its FWD non-GAAP PEG ratio at 0.07x while the sector median stands at 1.27x. Now this suggests that there is a 94% discount relative to the sector median.

When I compare Sandisk Corporation with some of its peers, SNDK still stands out. For instance, while the FWD non-GAAP PEG for SNDK is 0.07x, Western Digital (WDC) has its ratio at 0.68x. On the other side, While SND has its FWD Non-GAAP P/E at 15.78x, WDC has its ratio at 30.38x. Almost double. Another Peer is Seagate Technology (STX). While SNDK has its FWD Non-GAAP PEG at 0.07x, STX has its ratio at same 0.68x while its FWD Non-GAAP P/E at 30.07x. This shows you that in as much all of these companies are trading below their sector median, SNDK stands out because it has the lowest ratios, implying huge discounts relative to the sector median.

Key Risks

The biggest risk I see for Sandisk is memory cycle oversupply. As I earlier mentioned, the NAND flash market historically flips between shortage and glut. And if competitors such as WDC, STX, SK Hynix rapidly ramp new fabs or if SNDK overbuilds capacity, then prices could collapse. And this means that a sudden move from tight supply to oversupply would hit SNDK's profit margins hard.

The other risk is demand slowdown. While AI remains hot, growth could slowdown. And this implies that any pause in hyperscaler spending would reduce demand. SNDK's stock is priced for continued acceleration so even moderate slowdown could pressure sales.

Final Thoughts

At the end of the day, SNDK focus on data center SSDs and long term contracts is paying off in record margins and cash flow. And given the still limited investment in new NAND capacity and the AI driven demand, SNDK should continue to enjoy higher prices and accelerating profit growth. I have demonstrated that the stock does not appear richly valued and is trading at a discount relative to sector medians. And this is why I am comfortable rating Sandisk Corporation a Strong Buy.

data.title: WTV: A Meticulously Crafted Value-Themed ETF That Is Outperforming Its Larger Peers
data.description:
The WisdomTree U.S. Value Fund (WTV) is a $2.6B-sized niche ETF that focuses on US stocks with high shareholder yields (a metric which takes into account not just the dividends paid by a company, but the share repurchases it has engaged in, relative to its total market-cap) and strong quality characteristics. Put simply, this is a product that doesn’t just strive to deliver ample capital returns, but solid income as well. Also, if a company is scoring pretty high on the shareholder yield front, it also typically points to an operating model that is efficient enough to churn out a high degree of free cash flow every year, as the war chest of dividends and buybacks usually stems from this.

Key Characteristics of The WisdomTree U.S. Value Fund

What’s interesting to note is that WTV, which will wrap up two decades on the bourses in February next year, used to passively track an index called the WisdomTree U.S. LargeCap Value Index for the first decade of its lifetime. However, since December 2017, it has pivoted to adopting a ruled-based strategy in picking its stocks (more on this later). Since rules-based portfolios are quite strict with their benchmarking, one can expect these portfolios to see a lot of churn. In WTV’s case note that pretty much the entire portfolio gets turned over during a 1-year time frame (most ETFs only churn less than 1 in 3 stocks from their portfolio annually)

Despite shedding its erstwhile, passively managed stance, investors should note that the expense profile of this product, is still very affordable and in keeping with a passive ETF (at an expense ratio of 0.12%, it isn’t even one-fourth of the median expense rate).

As noted earlier, WTV’s quarterly income profile is one of its stronger suits, so long as one can ignore the sub-par yield of 1.79% (which is largely just a function of strong price appreciation). As you can see from the image below, WTV’s dividend growth rates over different time frames (annually, 3-year, 5-year or even 10-years) has consistently crossed the double-digit threshold, and has also been much superior to growth rates seen by other ETFs.

Investors will also be interested to note that over the last 3 years, WTV has provided bonus dividends in the final quarter of the year.

With regards to the texture of WTV’s portfolio (which currently consists of around 120 stocks), even though its average market-cap of over $40B would make it come across as a large-cap centric product, note that mid-caps dominate, with a 43% weight.

Sector-wise, those who like diversification will appreciate a product of this ilk, as it spreads its wings across 11 different sectors, with decent enough exposure towards all of them (even the smallest sector-materials, has a reasonable enough weight of almost 5%). Having said that, given that this is a valued-themed product, there’s little surprise to see a hardcore value sector-financials, accounting for the biggest stake although it is not overbearing (roughly one-fifth of the portfolio).

A Well-Rounded And Meticulous Portfolio Building Exercise

As noted earlier, WTV employs a rules-based model to build its portfolio and its starting universe of stocks consists of the 800 largest mid and large-cap businesses. In the next stage, WTV sets to out to assign a composite score (which is a function of quality as well as the shareholder yield, both of which receive equal weightage). During this second process, efforts are taken to eradicate stocks that are used to reducing their shares outstanding (diluters or low conviction buyback plays), those that are in the bottom 70% of the shareholder yield readings, and those who have low composite risk scores (or stocks that could be value traps).

Note that this composite “risk” score [CRS] is different from the composite score mentioned in the previous paragraph, as the former seeks to gauge both quality, as well as momentum (the latter which comes into consideration later on in the portfolio building process looks at quality and shareholder yield).

Quality is ascertained by a combination of static and dynamic readings on metrics such as the ROA, ROE, Cash flow over assets, and gross profit over assets. The momentum quotient is ascertained on how well these stocks have fared over the past 6m and 12 months after taking into consideration the volatility those stocks have experienced.

Stocks with high quality readings and high momentum readings will naturally have a superior CRS, and those that are in the bottom 20% are eradicated. Also, stocks that are in the top 5% by dividend yield, and the bottom half of CRS are also eradicated.

In the final stage of the portfolio building process, the 120 stocks with the highest composite scores (quality and shareholder yields) make the cut, with weighting of the stocks taking place on the basis of their shareholder yields. The fund manager does have the liberty to also facilitate certain sector tilts if required. Note that this portfolio gets rebalanced 4 times in a year.

How Has WTV Fared Over Time?

So, has WTV’s meticulous portfolio construction paid off in terms of returns? Yes, indeed, and what’s striking is that it has managed to comfortably outperform the three largest US value ETFs, namely the Vanguard Value ETF (VTV), the iShares Russell 1000 Value ETF (IWD), and the iShares S&P 500 Value ETF (IVE) by anything from 1.16x to 1.46x.

However, total returns only tell you one part of the story; investors should also consider looking at the Sharpe ratio, which shows you how well these products have fared against risk-free assets, after incorporating the risk they have encountered. Here, while WTV isn’t best-in-class (that honor goes to VTV the largest value ETF), it’s reading is certainly not bad.

The broad takeaway from the two images are that while WTV does do a great job from a return angle, it also tends to take on a bit more risk than its larger peer to facilitate those heightened returns.

A Few Reasons To Consider WTV Now

Firstly, while WTV may emphasize quality and shareholder yields, don’t also forget that at heart, this is a value-centric product, and value-based themes look set to make up for lost time, at the expense of growth. Themes of mean reversion tend to play out over decades in financial markets and over the last decade, we’ve seen the Russell 1000 value-growth ratio move further away from its mean. One could argue that this theme now looks stretched to the downside, with the current value to growth ratio at levels that are roughly only half its long-term mean of nearly 1x. Surely, value is due some catch up?

Value-based themes could also get some support from growing prospects of a rate hike in 2026 (something that was seen as unthinkable a few months back), a corollary of the ongoing war and its effects on the energy supply chain. If the benchmark interest rate were to rise, this would raise the discount rate at which the future cash flows of growth companies are discounted, leading to lower present values of these companies.

Then take note of WTV’s own compelling valuations, not only against the main equity benchmark (as represented by SPY), but also against the most popular value ETF- VTV. Whichever metric you look at, be it earnings, book value or cash flow, WTV’s multiple is the cheapest.

It also helps that WTV tilts mostly to the financial sector which is currently the cheapest sector around at just 14.4x P/E.

Just because it is cheap, investors shouldn’t make the mistake of assuming that the financials sector is set-up for bland growth. When we come up against Q1 earnings later this month, don’t be surprised to see these financial stocks posting some relatively strong earnings growth numbers. Already Factset expectations suggest that only the tech and materials sectors are poised to deliver superior earnings growth than the financial sector.

I also think that the risk-reward on WTV’s charts look a tad better now than it did in February, when the ETF had crossed the $101 levels and was trading outside its upper bollinger band. Buying then was always going to be a dicey proposition, but we have since seen some useful retracement, leading to a more favorable entry price.

Closing Thoughts

I would recommend WTV as it ticks a number of boxes-a rigorous screening mechanism (which has translated to compelling returns over time), a solid distribution track record, and a diversified yet favorable sector postioning. I also feel that value-based themes could be due a comeback, and WTV’s compelling valuations (even against prominent value ETFs), make it stand out even more. After looking overbought in February, WTV has also seen some useful retracement in its price action. WTV is a buy.

data.title: Dow Jones And U.S. Stock Market NFP Levels: Wall Street Scrambles For Impossible Certainty After The April Fool's Fakeout
data.description:
By Elior Manier

Certainty is a fool's errand in recent market dynamics.

The most seasoned traders would even admit that trading has never been about certainty. Still, more about careful planning and risk-taking - the former part is quite a daunting task with fundamentals changing by the minute.

The latter, however, is where traders can extract alpha by controlling their bias, sizing, and jumping ship with every market-changing headline.

Investors are looking for a dip to buy to profit from a real turn in the war. Still, despite better hopes for conflict resolution in the past week and a half, the overarching theme is one of fragile stability. De-escalation hasn't yet materialized with Iran multiplying attacks, reportedly now directly targeting US companies (including Amazon Web Services servers).

On the other hand, US and Israeli attacks on IRGC infrastructures continue - nothing really changed there. But the largest panic component came after President Trump's latest address at the White House yesterday, where he U-turned on his prior softer tone.

And that turn wasn't welcomed by broader assets, which all tumbled, including stock markets and futures around the globe, right as his speech started.

He did not mention a direct ground operation. Still, the Pentagon has been preparing for weeks of limited ground operation, which corroborates the deployment of Marines to the Middle East throughout last week.

That doesn't bode well for any hopes of de-escalation.

Once again, the only real element that traders should watch is oil and its price movements. Before hoping for the best, WTI would have to remain below $100 on a daily close, and a weekly close would be even better.

In any case, market volatility has been fragile, as volumes are lower amid the Passover and Easter holidays approaching, compounded by tomorrow's NFP release (preview incoming).

After dumping during overnight futures trading, stock markets gapped lower at the open but have corrected most of the move as frantic algorithms and traders rush to close positions ahead of major risk events ahead, not even counting the long weekend!

To gauge today’s market direction, let’s examine the intraday charts and trading levels for the major US indexes: the Dow Jones, Nasdaq, and S&P 500.

Current Session's Stock Heatmap

After consecutive positive sessions, the picture has changed quite drastically. Now mostly red, only a few smaller names are in the green.

Tesla (TSLA) is leading mega caps lower as Elon Musk officially filed the SpaceX IPO, expected to be the most expensive IPO ever.

For the rest, apart from Healthcare tumbling in harmony, the selloff is erratic, pointing at broad index selling.

Dow Jones 4H Chart and Trading Levels

The Dow broke out of its war downtrend, but the current market conditions are more mixed than bullish.

Despite a break retest attempt, a full break above 47,000 will be required to push for a more bullish action ahead. Markets will be closed tomorrow, so traders will have to look at futures to get their guides to the action:

  • A daily close above 47,000 brings back the rebound attempt
  • Falling back below 45,800, however, hints at further downside ahead.

Dow Jones Technical Levels for Trading:

Resistance Levels

  • Session highs 46,900
  • Pivotal resistance 47,000 +/- 100 points (bullish above)
  • Minor resistance 47,500-47,650
  • Key resistance at 48,000

Support Levels

  • 45,700-45,900 momentum pivot
  • January 2025 highs and war lows 45,280
  • Channel and morning lows 44,840
  • Next minor support 44,200-44,500
  • Major support 43,500-43,750

Nasdaq 4H Chart and Trading Levels

Nasdaq also erased most of its losses, getting back to a more neutral momentum, but will have to break and close above 24,000 after NFP to confirm chances of a prolonged rebound.

  • 24,200 breaking would confirm the bullish momentum
  • Falling back below 23,600, on the other hand, puts bears back in control

Nasdaq Technical Levels of Interest:

Resistance Levels

  • Major 2026 range lows 23,800-24,000
  • 24,200 (bullish above)
  • 24,450-25,550 resistance
  • Key Resistance 25,000-25,200 (range highs - long-term bullish above)

Support Levels

  • August 2025 pivot 23,500-23,650 (bearish below)
  • 22,900-23,000 higher time frame major support
  • 22,600 August 2025 support zone
  • Early 2025 ATH at 22,000-22,229 support

S&P 500 4H Chart and Trading Levels

The S&P 500 is remaining solidly in its war bear channel, which is the most consistent factor to keep your eyes on in order to determine if bulls are back in control or not (implying that for now, they still are not).

  • For the spoose, bulls will want to see a break above 6,600
  • Bears, on the other hand, will want to maintain the bear channel and see a daily close below 6,500.

S&P 500 Technical Levels of Interest:

Resistance Levels

  • 6,570-6,600 main pivotal resistance
  • 6,680-6,700 mini-resistance
  • 6,740 Key intraday resistance
  • Pivotal resistance 6,770-6,800

Support Levels

  • 6,490-6,512 October lows pivot
  • 6,442 past week dip
  • 6,360-6,380 key August 2025 support
  • 6,300 psychological level channel lows
  • January 2025 ATH 6,152

Safe trades, and keep track of headlines and Bitcoin over the weekend!

Original Post

data.title: Knot Offshore Partners LP Common: Focus On The Expected Recovery
data.description:
Knot Offshore Partners LP Common Units (KNOP) have really not participated in the recent rally caused by higher oil and gas prices. The stock price is up from the last article as this industry has its own recovery underway that is different from upstream. This article will attempt to further delineate what is going on with the cyclical recovery. The strong buy is likely still merited until the recovery is well underway.

Knot is in the business of essentially providing some needed services or assets to the offshore industry. That industry is currently experiencing the beginning of a recovery that resulted in an offer to essentially take the company private by its parent company. Anyone following Ken Fischer's books on "Super Stocks" or David Dreman's "Contrarian Investment" series of books can easily summarize that insiders often make an acquisition offer either before a downturn bottom or right after the downturn. It is a very common event. In this case, the offer came from the controlling company which knows the business extremely well (cyclical nature and all).

The thing to realize about any offer is that the party making the offer expects to make a profit. In this case, the party making the offer is very knowledgeable about a business that it essentially manages for the shareholders. This implies that the common units got so cheap that the company could make an offer to buy the stock and still make a profit in the future. In simple terms, the stock got too cheap due to some sort of market miscalculation about the value of the shares. That miscalculation (or valuation disagreement) resulted in an offer to take the company private.

Obviously, the offer did not go through. Someone else (hopefully a lot of someone "elses") realized that there was a market valuation issue compared to the outlook of the company and a potential valuation of the stock going forward.

Oftentimes the market tries to value a stock on the average profitability of the stock price throughout the business cycle. However, that valuation can get distorted (as it frequently does) because the market gets too pessimistic about future prospects during a cyclical downturn while getting too optimistic about future prospects near the top of the market. So, the difference between "perfect information" theories and actual performance can be "eye popping".

In the academic world, anyone who has studied economics even a little bit knows that the basis for economics is that man is a rational human being. But when you go to psychology, that part of the academic world knows differently. But economists have long complained that the equations used to predict what will happen become too complicated if psychology must be incorporated in the equations (and in the case of the stock market, the correct valuation of stocks). Since finance and hence the stock market is a subsection of economics in the academic world, this poses a limit as to what economics can do.

So, these offers when stock prices are in the mispriced range are likely to keep coming in the foreseeable future because information is imperfect and because economics and psychology are likely to remain completely separate in the future. That is before you get to how humans really behave in the real world (which is all those theories applied to get to reality).

In the case of Knot, one would have to believe that the insiders that manage the company have a firm believe that the current situation is the beginning of a whole lot of earnings improvement to justify the expenses of going private. Those benefits must exceed what the market thinks about the future enough for there to be a reasonable chance to make a profit.

My last article in March 2025, stated that a long-delayed recovery was underway. The fact that the stock is up about 60% since then now has a boost in the form of an offer to purchase the outstanding stock even if that offer did not succeed. That boost comes from not only the potential offering price; but also, from the profit the entity making the offer expected to make.

Now maybe not all that is known. But an individual investor is free to make assumptions of his own about the situation. The conclusion has to be that there will be better times ahead in a known cyclical industry that exceed the current market value of the future.

Understand that the market that Knot serves is the offshore business. That business can be cyclical. But that cycle is often different from the upstream business many are familiar with. So, supply can exceed demand (or the opposite). But like any cyclical entity, sooner or later supply and demand come back into balance. Then the imbalance often heads in the other direction to provide a period of prosperity or a cyclical bottom.

But this means that the cyclical past is probably not a good predictor of what may happen in the future. It is extremely hard to predict what rates and hence cash flow (debt servicing and other related things like dividends) will be like several years from now based upon what happened in the past.

Instead, it is probably just as good to simply assume that the past stock price highs will increase by the rate of inflation plus some business growth per share as well. This does depend upon a satisfactory evaluation of management. Since this is very subjective, a wide range of possibilities may appeal to many different investors.

But the key for a company like this (where finances are fine and the parent company will step in to keep things fine or at least it has in the past), is to purchase the stock at a bargain price such that a sale will produce a reasonable profit.

Unlike many, I personally believe the old Wall Street saying about "sometimes the bulls win and sometimes the bears win. But the pigs never win so do not be a pig". To me this means what many great investors state which is be consistent about making profits rather than getting every last penny. That is because getting every last penny risks an overstay that more than wipes out any profit (to end up with losses instead).

Risks

There is really a lack of academic research in this area because it would mean that economics and psychology would have to work together for a statistically reliable outcome. Instead, we as public recipients of such research often end up relying upon casual outcomes (that are supposed to be statistically significant) rather than the causal relationships that would result if the two disciplines would work together. Casual relationships are ones that work "until they do not" without theoretical backing.

Since this has long been the case probably since before I was born (and its widely accepted this is the case), there is a lack of research on the proper procedure, the process, and the success of the potential outcome.

Finance does provide some research on stock price returns in given situations. But that is far from what is necessary for the professional or even the individual investor.

However, it does seem to be relevant that an industry insider with a fair amount of knowledge of the business made an offer to acquire the stock. But given all of the above suppositions, there is absolutely no reason to assume that insider is either correct or has a good idea of the profitability of the situation. There could be other potential offers or situations that prove to be more profitable. It is all really a professional guess at this point.

It could even be wrong that the coming recovery will continue with sufficient vigor to fund that anticipated "double profit" the offer would seem to indicate. The whole situation could be vastly underestimated as well.

The unknowns would seem to indicate that a situation like this should be part of a basket of similar stocks to reduce the risk. Another words, rather than one stock with a turnaround (or cyclical recovery) idea, I prefer a basket of small positions equal to that one position to reduce the risk.

That makes proper management evaluation a key risk as well in this process.

Summary

Many times, a knowledgeable professional in the industry has a dispute with the market about the future value of a stock price. If the difference is great enough, one of the results of this may be an offer for all of the outstanding stocks. That appears to be what happened here.

As a result, Knop remains a strong buy in that the recovery underway is still apparently in its early stages. That is usually what an offer indicates. But that means there is several years of recovery ahead.

The final valuation of the stock price will depend upon the industry standing "when we get there". Nonetheless, a basket of similarly chosen stocks should treat an investor well. This is that rare out-of-favor stock in a fairly frothy market.

Generally, in this situation, I consider this stock that rare strong buying opportunity in what I consider to be an overpriced market. Since I also consider the recovery in its early stages, I would hold the stock until business rates (for this business) are sky high and everyone is optimistic they would go higher. That to me would indicate a cyclical top when it is time to form an exit strategy.

data.title: Politics And The Markets 04/03/26
data.description:
This is the forum for daily political discussion on Seeking Alpha. A new version is published every market day.

Please don't leave political comments on other articles or posts on the site.

The comments below are not regulated with the same rigor as the rest of the site, and this is an 'enter at your own risk' area as discussion can get very heated. If you can't stand the heat... you know what they say...

More on Today's Markets:

Moderation Guidelines:

We remove comments under the following categories:

  • Personal attacks on another user account
  • Anti-Vaxxer or covid related misinformation
  • Stereotyping, prejudiced or racist language about individuals or the topic under discussion.
  • Inciting violence messages, encouraging hate groups and political violence.

Regardless of which side of the political divide you find yourself, please be courteous and don't direct abuse at other users.

For any issue with regards to comments please email us at : moderation@seekingalpha.com.

Seeking Alpha's Disclosure: Past performance is no guarantee of future results. No recommendation or advice is being given as to whether any investment is suitable for a particular investor. Any views or opinions expressed above may not reflect those of Seeking Alpha as a whole. Seeking Alpha is not a licensed securities dealer, broker or US investment adviser or investment bank. Our analysts are third party authors that include both professional investors and individual investors who may not be licensed or certified by any institute or regulatory body.

data.title: NewLake Capital Partners: Collect A Double-Digit Yield From This Cannabis Landlord
data.description:
I remember 2018 fondly, it was the year I began my career as an equities analyst. It was also the year that marijuana stocks were making moonshot gains every other week.

Canada legalized marijuana for recreational use that year, and Wall Street anticipated that American federal legalization would be just around the corner.

Today, about eight years later, federal legalization might be closer than it was in 2018 but it’s not here yet. Despite that, I think NewLake Capital Partners (NLCP) is worth serious consideration for your dividend portfolio.

And it’s not just because its yield is just shy of 12% at current prices. Though, that does make it pretty attractive to be perfectly honest.

Let’s get into it.

The Opportunity: Prohibition Nearing its End?

NewLake isn’t one of the cannabis companies that popped in 2018 only to crash shortly thereafter. It’s a real estate investment trust or REIT that rents out retail properties to state-licensed cannabis companies.

And even if investors might have jumped the gun in 2018 I do think federal legalization is coming, just more slowly than many hoped it would eight years ago.

Because, despite still being illegal at the federal level, cannabis is now fully legal for adult use in 24 states, legal for medical use in 15 states, and the use of CBD for medical purposes is legal in 6 states. Only Kansas, Wyoming, and Idaho have no state-level laws on the books offering the drug greater legality than it has on the federal level.

At the end of 2018, marijuana was only fully legal in 10 states so that number has more than doubled in the almost-decade since.

Beyond that, according to a Pew Research poll, 57% of American adults believe marijuana should be legal for both medical and recreational use while 32% of American adults think it should at least be legal for medical use.

Only 11% of American adults think marijuana should remain totally banned.

So, while the federal government is moving much slower than opinions on the ground and state legislatures (no big surprise there,) legalization is likely inevitable at this point.

And in a bit of a surprise move, President Trump has made some moves in the first two years of his second term to legalize marijuana.

In December of 2025, President Trump signed executive order EO 143709 which is meant to reschedule marijuana from a schedule 1 narcotic to a schedule 3.

Schedule 1 drugs are substances with no medical use and a high potential for abuse like heroin or ecstasy. Funny enough, marijuana is a schedule 1 drug while cocaine is schedule 2 which is considered less dangerous. Adderall and Ritalin are also schedule 2 drugs.

Schedule 3 drugs on the other hand are where you’ll find things your doctor might prescribe you like Codeine. You can’t go buy them off the shelf like Tylenol but they do have recognized medical uses.

Given that the executive order I mentioned recognizes marijuana’s pain treatment capabilities in the first paragraph, it makes sense to recognize that in law.

Anyway, the opportunity with NewLake is that you can profit from the cannabis industry without the risks of investing in one of the retailers a lot of people got burned by when their shares fell off a cliff in 2019 and 2021.

Instead of a high risk, high reward startup, New Lake is a slow-and-steady dividend payer better geared to play the glacial pace at which the federal government is prone to move.

And, based on NewLake’s latest results, that is still a very profitable industry to be in.

The Money: This Won’t be Mary Jane’s Last Dance

Let’s start with NewLake’s real estate portfolio. It operates 1.7 million square feet of space across 12 states between 15 cultivation facilities and 19 dispensaries. In total it has 34 properties and leases 31 of them out which gives it a 91% occupancy rate. The average lease on NewLake’s properties is 12 years.

Its largest tenant is Curaleaf (CURLF) which makes up 25.3% of NewLake’s revenue, however, it has a fairly well diversified group of 13 tenants in total including Cresco Labs (CRLBF) and Trulieve (TCNNF).

The company’s revenue has grown slowly and steadily. For 2025 it brough in $51 million in revenue, up just shy of 2% from 2024 and it has a fantastic net profit margin of 52.42%. And, NewLake has incredibly low debt which is something I always love to see. Its total debt to equity ratio is sitting at 0.02 right now.

NewLake's funds from operation or FFO grew steadily in 2025, up 2.5% over 2024 which is just the latest in a long-term trend of steady growth. From 2023 to 2024, the company's FFO grew 2.5% as well, 2022 to 2023 saw growth of 11%, and from 2021 to 2022 the company's FFO grew 78%.

So with the initial rapid growth phase over, the company has settled into what looks like a stable, long-term growth pattern.

Now, on to the reason you usually hold a REIT, the dividend.

NewLake pays a dividend of $1.72 per share annually, which is good for a yield of 11.91% at current prices. It has raised that dividend every year since its IPO in 2021, it’s up 79% in that time frame.

However, since its first full year as a public company in 2022, the yield has always been incredibly high. It was 8.99% that year and right now at 11.91% it’s closer to its all-time high of 12.63% than it has been since 2023.

Now, in terms of share price, things have been a little rough for NewLake to say the least. Over the past year it’s down 0.07% and year-to-date it’s down 9.8%. But that’s good for investors looking to scoop up dividend stocks with the highest yield possible.

And, given NewLake’s solid financial position, high profitability, and incredibly low debt, I don’t think it or its dividend are going anywhere anytime soon.

The drop in share price has also created a fantastic value proposition here as well. NewLake’s price to FFO ratio is sitting at 7.16 which is 45.8% lower than the median for its sector.

Speaking of its price, I expect NewLake to hold fairly steady at its current $14-$15 price. The FFO estimate for the end of 2026 is $1.87, if we multiply that by the company's forward price to FFO ratio of 7.73 we get a price target of $14.45 so it's likely the company will maintain its high yield for some time to come.

Note, it did miss its Q4 2025 estimate by 2.25% but it did beat estimates in the previous three quarters by a margin of 4.49% for Q1, 17.98% for Q2 and 12.94% for Q3 so one missed quarter in the last year is not too much of a concern by my math. .

Moving out further, the 2027 FFO estimate is $1.94 and the forward price to FFO for that year is 7.45. Multiplying those together, we get a price target of, again, $14.45. So, it seems the market has settled on a fair price for the company where it's at now.

Even the highest price target set by analysts only has NewLake shares hitting $16 by the end of 2027 which is entirely possible. Regardless of what the share prices do exactly, most REITs do not have crazy price appreciation so you'll be making your return on the near-12% yield.

Now, compared to its fellow cannabis REIT Innovative Industrial Properties (IIPR), NewLake doesn’t look like an obvious improvement at first glance. The main reason being its yield is far lower than Innovative Industrial’s which is sitting at 15.15% right now.

However, where NewLake shines is in its dividend safety and consistency. While high payout ratios are nothing new for REITs, they do have to payout 90% of their taxable income as dividends after all, Innovative Industrial’s AFFO payout ratio is at 104% which is concerning while NewLake’s is a much more manageable 90.4%.

In terms of consistency, NewLake wins as well. It has paid a dividend to its share holders for four years in a row and it has raised it in every single one of those years. Innovative Industrial on the other hand has paid a dividend for eight years and only raised it consecutively for one year. It actually cut its Q4 2024 dividend significantly, from $1.90 to $1.07.

So, NewLake has a safer dividend it has raised consistently and never cut. The safety of it makes up for the missing yield in my view.

And, while Innovative Industrial is still very profitable, it’s worth noting its net income margin is lower at 44.46%.

Finally, right now NewLake is very oversold with an RSI of 28.9. That likely means its headed for a reversal in the near future which is likely to drop the yield. However, I think NewLake will maintain a solid yield even if it’s not close to 12% like it is now.

Putting it all together, we have a financially stable REIT with steadily growing revenue, high profit margins, a fantastic yield, and very, very little debt which has also raised its dividend consistently since its IPO. That sounds like a winner to me.

Now, on to the catalyst and the risk which are pretty much one in the same.

The Risk and Catalyst: A Hazy Future?

The catalyst that would send NewLake and all stocks involved in the cannabis industry (even those indirectly involved like NewLake) into the stratosphere would be federal cannabis legalization.

Federal legalization would make it far easier for companies involved in the cannabis industry to operate, primarily in terms of access to banking services and credit. Because Cannabis is a schedule 1 drug federally, cannabis companies have a difficult time accessing banks and credit as banks could be prosecuted if they worked with them.

Because of that, cannabis business operate in cash for the most part.

If cannabis were legalized nationwide and companies operating in the industry were to gain access to banking and credit like every other business, then not only would it benefit banks with more customers, it would also allow cannabis companies to access lines of credit to expand more quickly.

Expansion in the cannabis business requires more real estate like dispensaries or hydroponic growing facilities. And that in turn provides an opportunity for NewLake to expand. It would probably also make it easier to collect rent from its tenants.

The flipside of that is, if cannabis continues to operate in the legal grey area it’s in right now, then its growth as an industry will continue to be hamstrung by a lack of credit and the legal weirdness of its current state.

And, while it’s unlikely, if any of the states that have legalized marijuana were to reverse course and switch back to prohibition of the drug then it would be a worrying sign for NewLake and the cannabis industry to put it very mildly.

However, I find that unlikely given public opinion toward marijuana as I mentioned above as well as the federal government’s pivot toward rescheduling marijuana and pushing for decriminalization.

In fact, there are a couple of bills that have been floated in congress over the past few years like the SAFE Banking Act and the SAFER Banking Act that would make it much easier for banks to interact with companies in the cannabis business.

It’s moving slowly and is likely to continue on at this glacial pace, but it is moving…

The Verdict: Bet on the Tortoise

And that’s part of what makes a dividend cannabis play like NewLake so appealing. You can profit from the industry in a much more passive way, not tying your money up in high-risk startups.

No, you can collect a dividend while you wait for the federal government to finally catch up with where the states and the average American have been for a few years now.

The eager cannabis investors back in 2018 had the right idea, there is serious potential here. But they bet on the hare in the old fable, the small startup companies that burst out of the gate but ran out of steam quickly.

I think the better way to play cannabis is to bet on the tortoise. Slow and steady wins the race or so they saying goes. And New Lake is certainly steady although its growth is a good deal quicker than the walking pace of that tortoise Aesop wrote about.

NewLake is a buy in my opinion, and a good candidate for a dividend reinvestment plan you can let compound for years to come.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Eldorado Gold: 40% Production Growth Trading Under 8x Earnings
data.description:
El Dorado Gold Update

El Dorado Gold (EGO) is a name I've covered on Seeking Alpha for nearly a decade on Seeking Alpha. Well, some things never change: this is still a company with strong assets that the market isn't giving it enough credit for, held back by previous permitting delays in Greece, and a stock that is well overdue for a re-rating.

Several catalysts are finally here. For one, Skouries is just months away from first production, while mines like Lamaque keep on delivering. Yet, the stock still trades at a discount to many of its peers.

Eldorado reminds me of B2Gold (BTG) in many ways, as they're both mid-tier gold miners currently trading at very low price-to-earnings multiples, with clear 2026 catalysts potentially leading to a re-rating of their stocks. The difference in the two names is jurisdiction risk and growth outlook. B2Gold continues to work through a messy year with problems in Mali and ramp-up issues at the Goose mine, while El Dorado works through bringing the Skouries project into production and growing total mine output 40% over the next two years. Finding this combination of growth and cheapness in the same stock doesn't happen too often.

The company also has strong momentum, recently deliveringstrong 2025 results with production at the upper-end of its guidance. More promising is the fact that Q4 was its best quarter of the year, with earnings per share of $.63 beating analysts' estimates of $.60. And, with gold around $4,700/oz currently, its cash flow is off the charts.

Here's a summary of why I think El Dorado Gold is a BUY at current prices.

The Growth Pipeline Changes Everything

I've been a follower of the Skouries project for years, and have always loved the upside potential, especially its potential to produce metals at super-low costs. It's hard to believe that it might finally be in production soon. I'll admit, there were times in the past when I wondered if we would ever get to this stage. But, here we are in 2026, with construction nearly completed and close to $1 billion in capital spent to date. First production is finally scheduled for early Q3 2026, with commercial production to follow shortly thereafter. Once in full production, this mine should add 140,000ozAu per year, plus 67 million pounds of copper, with total AISC among the lowest in its portfolio.

Lamaque has been a steady producer for El Dorado in the meantime. I wrote about Eldorado's Lamaque acquisition back when they bought Integra Gold for C$590 million in 2017. I previously thought the deal was more favorable to Integra shareholders. Well, I'm happy to eat crow here, because Lamaque has become one of the best underground gold mines in Canada. Not to mention, the massive increase in gold prices over the past 9 years has added enormous value to the asset.

Olympias is getting a mill expansion to 650 ktpa with commissioning expected in H2 2026. Kisladag in Turkey is a strong producer with improvements underway. Also, investors shouldn't forget about the Perama Hill project. El Dorado is still working through permitting this mine, and in a best case scenario, it would be another producing mine in just a few years time, but it's not included in the current guidance figures.

Costs And Margins

El Dorado isn't a super low cost producer, but it's somewhere in the middle of the pack among its gold mining peers (GDX). For 2026, we should see all-in costs fall between $1,670 to $1,870/oz (note that Skouries will be reported separately while it ramps up).

The margins should still be huge. Currently, El Dorado has gross profit margins just under 45%. But, at gold around $4,700, the midpoint AISC of $1,770/oz, implies a margin of close to $3,000 per ounce. We should see an increase in margins and huge free cash flow based on where gold prices are trading, its 540,000ozAu production, and estimated cash costs. Free cash flow should only grow higher as Skouries starts contributing, and pulls down its overall costs.

The copper upside you get with El Dorado is worth mentioning here, too. Skouries, once in production, should produce 20-40 million pounds of copper in 2026, and ramp up in 2027 and beyond. Copper prices are $5.62/lb, and many feel prices are likely to remain strong and possibly go even higher from here. Demand continues to be driven by AI data center buildouts. When I wrote about Kinross Gold (KGC) last month, I flagged the Lobo-Marte copper optionality as underappreciated. Same logic applies here. Having copper exposure layer onto a gold-focused portfolio at this point in the cycle is a bonus the market isn't pricing.

Valuation: The Best Part Of The Story

The investment case in El Dorado would be strong if the stock were fairly valued. The fact that it's undervalued makes the investment case even sweeter. Check out Seeking Alpha's valuation grade of A- above, with several metrics grading as A or A+.

At its current stock price, Eldorado trades at around 7.4x its 2026 consensus earnings. That's based on an average EPS estimate of $4.67, according to the 6 analysts covering the stock. Look out to 2027, when Skouries is contributing a full year and gold output is approaching 700,000 ounces, and that multiple falls to below 5x.

The company's consensus price to NAV is 0.60x, cheaper than SSR Mining (SSRM), Equinox Gold (EQX), IAMGOLD (IAG), and Alamos Gold (AGI). Only B2Gold trades lower among its peers listed above, and for a good reason: B2Gold has the Mali overhang and Goose ramp-up issues. On EV/EBITDA, Eldorado sits at roughly 4x 2026 estimates, which is obviously quite low.

Capital returns are picking up too. The newly added $0.075 per share quarterly dividend was recently announced and begun in Q1. Share buybacks are also part of its capital allocation strategy, with $204 million in stock repurchased and cancelled during 2025 (and, I'll add, at much lower stock prices).

The strong balance sheet means El Dorado can easily fund its capital return programs. As of its last financial report, it has $869 million in cash against $500 million in senior notes (6.25% interest rate, due Sept. 1, 2029), and roughly $800 million in Skouries project debt, which is secured by Skouries and Hellas Gold assets (non-recourse debt to El Dorado), and will be repaid in 13 semi-annual installments from Dec. 31 of this year to Dec. 31, 2032.

That's manageable for a company generating this level of cash flow — $740+ million in annual operating cash flow — and it gets a lot more comfortable once Skouries turns from a capital consumer to a producer.

The Foran Mining Deal

One more thing to flag. Eldorado has announced the acquisition of Foran Mining, with its key asset being the McIlvenna Bay copper project in Saskatchewan.

Closing is expected Q2 2026; should this deal go through, the newly combined company is targeting roughly 900,000 gold-equivalent ounces with approximately $2.1 billion in EBITDA by 2027. It further tilts the portfolio toward copper and Canadian jurisdiction exposure. I plan to provide more coverage of the new company once the deal closes.

Key Risks to Consider

Skouries execution is the big one. This project is no stranger to delays. And while Q3 2026 first production still looks achievable given all of the construction progress we've seen, keep an eye out for any further delays that could push production out.

Turkey is not considered a "Tier-1" mining jurisdiction like the US, Canada, and Australia are. Kisladag and Efemcukuru are solid operators, but Turkey has a habit of changing the rules. Higher royalty rates enacted recently are already hitting the cost line, and roughly a third of current production sits in that jurisdiction. I'd feel better about Eldorado's risk profile if the Turkish weighting were smaller.

Gold price sensitivity matters. A correction to $4,000 keeps margins healthy. At $3,500 things get tighter, particularly at the higher-cost operations. The growth thesis also needs sustained copper prices for the full Skouries value to materialize.

Bottom Line

Eldorado Gold is one of the better setups I see in the mid-tier gold space right now. For a market cap of $7.2 billion, investors are getting a solid gold miner with a clear path to 40% production growth in just a few years at a great valuation. I don't think the valuation reflects a lot of the upside: Under 8x current-year earnings and below 5x on a 2027 look-through are cheap for a company executing this well.

I don't like it quite as much as B2Gold at current prices, where the discount is more extreme and the re-rating potential is bigger if Goose and Fekola normalize. But I'd take Eldorado over many of its larger peers trading at higher multiples and slower growth. Kinross at 10x earnings is a fine stock, but it doesn't have this kind of near-term production inflection. At $34, Eldorado should re-rate higher as Skouries delivers, the Foran Mining deal closes, and the 2027 numbers become impossible to ignore.

data.title: GameStop Is Fairly Valued And It's Now Focused On M&A
data.description:
I still think GameStop (GME) may achieve a successful turnaround. My original thesis was that GameStop was a turnaround stock because it started selling higher-margin collectibles. I said that its pivot was working, but I still considered it a hold because it appeared slightly overvalued. But now, investors are interested in GameStop for another reason. GameStop has a lot of cash on its balance sheet and now plans to use its cash to buy another company. If that happens, investors could be looking at a very different company. So, in this update, I decided to investigate what would happen if GameStop did make a big acquisition. For the time being, though, GameStop’s valuation appears reasonable for its current gaming-store business.

GameStop closed or sold 998 stores in 2025, according to its 2025 10-K, and it now has 2,206 stores. So, both its revenue and its expenses are lower now. In Q4 2025, GameStop’s revenue fell 10.8% to $1.104 billion, but its gross profit rose 6.4% to $386.8 million. So, the retail stores’ margins are still improving. If you adjust for other factors such as fluctuations in bitcoin prices and tax benefits, it looks like GameStop’s pivot to collectible sales is still working fairly well. GameStop didn’t report Q4 comps or provide 2026 guidance, though, which is unusual for a retail store. But again, its investors might not care about that.

The most important thing in GameStop’s report is that the company has $6.30 billion in cash and $2.71 billion in marketable securities, so it effectively has $9.01 billion in cash right now. And that might allow it to buy a much larger company. So, that’s the first factor I’d consider in this update.

GameStop Might Be Able to Buy a Larger Company Than Investors Expect

GameStop also has $4.16 billion in long-term debt, so it has a net cash position of $4.85 billion. And it might be able to spend even more than that on an acquisition. I’ve seen other companies leverage themselves up to 4X net debt to EBITDA to finance acquisitions recently. GameStop’s 2025 EBITDA was $299 million, so it could have a net debt position of $1.196 billion while maintaining a ratio of 4X net debt to EBITDA. And if GameStop did buy another company, the new company’s net debt to EBITDA ratio would also include the target’s EBITDA as well.

Recently, there have been rumors that GameStop might buy Best Buy (BBY). Best Buy is a huge electronics store chain. So, I decided to check if this would even be possible. Best Buy has $2.602 billion in TTM EBITDA, so the combined company would have $2.901 billion in EBITDA. The merged company could borrow up to $11.604 billion, assuming a ratio of 4X net debt to EBITDA. Best Buy also has $1.89 billion in cash and $1.15 billion in long-term debt, so it has net cash of $740 million. Add that to GameStop’s $4.85 billion and the merged company would have $5.59 billion in net cash.

So, if GameStop merged with Best Buy, it would have $5.59 billion in cash to pay Best Buy’s shareholders. And it could also borrow another $11.604 billion, based on the merged company’s earning power. So, Best Buy could pay a total of $17.194 billion to Best Buy’s shareholders for their stock. Best Buy’s EV on April 2 was $15.69 billion. So, it looks like a leveraged buyout of Best Buy by GameStop might be doable. And if Ryan Cohen lowered Best Buy’s costs afterward, its EBITDA would rise, so the combined company might have even more borrowing capacity. It would be very speculative to invest in GameStop because an acquisition like this may be theoretically possible, but it does sound like investors are still discussing this possibility.

GameStop Is Planning to Buy A Company Soon

Even if GameStop doesn’t try to buy Best Buy, it does sound like it will buy a company in 2026. In its 10-K, the company said, “Shareholders should understand that our strategy is now explicitly focused on leveraging our cash, flexible capital structure, and stock to acquire assets that we believe will undergo a significant re-rating under our stewardship.” So, GameStop might also issue stock to acquire a company, and that would also make a large acquisition affordable. But it won’t need to do that unless it buys a very large company.

GameStop Isn’t Going to Close Any More Stores in 2026

In its 10-K, GameStop also said that it won’t close any more stores in 2026. So, GameStop’s revenue won’t drop in 2026 because of store closures, but its operating costs won’t fall further because of store closures either. But GameStop is still making efficiency improvements to its remaining stores, so its margins could improve a little bit in 2026 anyway.

GameStop didn’t provide any 2026 guidance in its 10-K, and it doesn’t report comps for its game stores either. So, there’s no official information that I can use to forecast GameStop’s 2026 sales. And it’s still in the middle of a pivot as well. Additionally, GameStop still doesn’t have any Wall Street analyst coverage. In my previous article, I projected that GameStop’s revenue would fall in 2026 because I expected it to close stores, and now that’s no longer true. But now it might be better to project that GameStop will report flat revenue growth in 2026.

GameStop’s Business Model Is a Risk Factor

GameStop brought up an unusual risk factor in its 10-K. Regulators might decide that it’s an investment company now. Right now, GameStop is classified as a retail store. But it’s speculating on cryptocurrency, which isn’t something that retail stores normally do. And it’s also holding billions of dollars in other securities. So, GameStop is not acting like a normal retail store in the mall, it’s acting like an investment firm. And federal regulators might force GameStop to obey the rules for investment firms if they decide that it’s not a retail store anymore.

GameStop’s Shareholders Might Not Approve Of Its Acquisition

Investors don’t like some of the big M&A deals that have happened recently. McCormick’s (MKC) recent deal with Unilever (UL) caused both companies’ stocks to drop. And Sysco’s (SYY) announcement that it was buying Jetro Restaurant Depot also didn’t go over well. So, if GameStop buys another large company, such as Best Buy, there’s a possibility that both companies’ stock prices will fall sharply. Regulators might also attempt to block the deal if GameStop attempts to buy a direct competitor such as Best Buy.

Updated Comparative Valuation for GameStop

I still think it’s probably better to use the EV/sales ratio for GameStop because its large cash balance affects its valuation. On April 2, GameStop had a TTM EV/sales of 1.53. Best Buy’s TTM EV/sales was 0.38, so GameStop was still trading at a premium to its closest peer. The sector median for consumer discretionary companies was 1.26, so GameStop was also trading at a premium to the sector as a whole. This metric includes operating leases, but the other companies’ EV figures also include their operating leases as well.

Updated GameStop Valuation

GameStop reported adjusted net income of $647.4 million in 2025. That’s a 17.8% adjusted net margin, which is high for a store, but it sounds like collectible sales have boosted this company’s margins. And again, I’m now projecting that GameStop’s revenue will be flat in 2026 because it stopped closing stores. Because GameStop didn’t provide any 2026 guidance, I’d project that its adjusted net margin and its profit will remain the same in 2026 as well. GameStop’s adjusted net income is higher than its GAAP income, which was $418.4 million in 2025. But the company did report a lot of one-time expenses related to its store closures, so it’s reasonable to use adjusted income here.

Again, there’s no forward P/E available for this stock because it has no analyst coverage. But right now, the median forward P/E for consumer discretionary companies is 16. So, with a 2026 P/E of 16 and adjusted net income of $647.4 million in 2026, GameStop would have a market cap of $10.358 billion. According to its 2025 10-K, GameStop had 447.6 million shares outstanding at the end of the year. And if I divide $10.358 billion by 447.6 million shares, I get a one-year target price of $23.14 per share.

GameStop’s current share price is $23.36, so my target price is flat compared to the current price. So, this company appears to be fairly valued right now.

Conclusion

I still think that GameStop’s turnaround is working. Based on its recent results, the company appears to be fairly valued if you value it like a normal retail store that’s pivoting. But it also appears that GameStop is planning to make an acquisition soon, and it might be a large acquisition. And that adds a lot of uncertainty. GameStop hasn’t identified a buyout target, so it’s not possible to predict what might happen. It might be better to wait and see what the company does. So, I’d say that GameStop is still a hold.
